program.name: Knee Armor Protocol
program.goal: Knee injury prevention for trail running

athlete.knee_history: Bilateral anterior + medial pain, 2x hyaluronic acid injections (2023, 2024)

baseline_tests.name: Single-Leg Wall Sit
baseline_tests.instructions: Back against wall, knee at 90 degrees, one foot off ground. Time each leg until failure.

baseline_tests.name: Single-Leg Step-Down
baseline_tests.instructions: Stand on 20cm step on one leg. Lower opposite heel to floor in 3 seconds. Count reps until form breaks (knee valgus, trunk lean, or balance loss).

baseline_tests.name: Single-Leg Calf Raise
baseline_tests.instructions: Stand on one leg on flat floor. Rise to full tiptoe, lower in 2 seconds. Count max reps until unable to reach full height.

baseline_tests.name: Knee Pain VAS
baseline_tests.instructions: Rate worst knee pain during or after last long run. 0 = no pain, 10 = worst imaginable.

exercises.name: Bike or Rower
exercises.notes: Easy effort, low resistance. Goal is synovial fluid circulation.

exercises.name: Bodyweight Squat to Box
exercises.notes: Sit back to bench or box at parallel depth. Knees track over toes.

exercises.name: Leg Press
exercises.notes: Feet shoulder width, mid-platform. Full ROM but stop before knee pain onset. Primary tendon loader.

progression.instruction: Add 5 kg if all sets completed at RPE ≤7
Continue adding 2.5-5 kg per session when possible

exercises.name: Spanish Squat
exercises.notes: Loop heavy band behind knees, anchored to rig. Lean back into band, squat to parallel. Keeps tibia vertical, loads quad tendon with minimal patellofemoral compression.

progression.instruction: Bodyweight only, find appropriate band tension
Hold dumbbell at chest to add load

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Barbell or dumbbells. Soft knee bend, hip hinge, feel hamstring stretch at bottom. Posterior chain balance, hamstrings as ACL agonist.

progression.instruction: Start ~30 kg barbell or 2x12 kg dumbbells
Add 2.5-5 kg when RPE drops below 7

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.notes: Rear foot on bench. Slight forward trunk lean. Eccentric emphasis mimics downhill braking in trail running.

progression.instruction: Add 2 kg per dumbbell when RPE allows

exercises.name: Side-Lying Hip Abduction (Cable)
exercises.notes: Cable at ankle, standing with slight forward trunk lean. Slow and controlled. Targets glute medius to reduce femoral internal rotation and medial knee stress.

progression.instruction: Increase resistance when 15 reps feels easy

exercises.name: Clamshell with Band (Feet Elevated)
exercises.notes: Feet on bench variation increases glute med activation by ~30% vs floor. Targets external rotators to protect against knee valgus.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Calf Raise (Step, Weighted)
exercises.notes: Stand on step edge, full ROM (drop heel below platform, rise to max). Hold dumbbell. Soleus + gastroc strength reduces load transferred to knee.

progression.instruction: Hold 8-12 kg dumbbell, add 2 kg when easy

exercises.name: Single-Leg Balance on BOSU (Eyes Closed)
exercises.notes: Flat side up. Proprioceptive challenge for trail terrain.

exercises.name: Quad + Hip Flexor Stretch
exercises.notes: Half-kneeling position, squeeze glute on back leg, lean forward gently. Important for patella tracking given tight hips.

evidence.citation: Kongsgaard M, Kovanen V, Aagaard P, et al. Corticosteroid injections, eccentric decline squat training and heavy slow resistance training in patellar tendinopathy. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2009;19(6):790-802.
evidence.claim: Heavy slow resistance training produced superior tendon adaptation (collagen synthesis, tendon structure) compared to corticosteroid injection at 12-month follow-up.

evidence.citation: Rio E, Kidgell D, Purdam C, et al. Isometric exercise induces analgesia and reduces inhibition in patellar tendinopathy. Br J Sports Med. 2015;49(19):1277-1283.
evidence.claim: Isometric quad contractions at ~70 degrees knee flexion produced immediate patellar tendon pain reduction and increased cortical drive to the quadriceps.

evidence.citation: Powers CM. The influence of abnormal hip mechanics on knee injury: a biomechanical perspective. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2010;40(2):42-51.
evidence.claim: Weak hip abductors and external rotators lead to femoral internal rotation and adduction, increasing lateral patellofemoral contact pressure and medial knee joint stress.

evidence.citation: Lack S, Barton C, Sohan O, Crossley K, Morrissey D. Proximal muscle rehabilitation is effective for patellofemoral pain: a systematic review with meta-analysis. Br J Sports Med. 2015;49(21):1365-1376.
evidence.claim: Hip and quadriceps strengthening combined produces greater pain reduction and functional improvement in patellofemoral pain than quadriceps strengthening alone.

evidence.citation: Selkowitz DM, Beneck GJ, Powers CM. Which exercises target the gluteal muscles while minimizing activation of the tensor fascia lata? Electromyographic assessment using fine-wire electrodes. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2013;43(2):54-64.
evidence.claim: Clamshell with feet elevated and side-lying hip abduction showed highest glute med to TFL activation ratio, making them preferred for selective glute med training.

evidence.citation: Hewett TE, Myer GD, Ford KR, et al. Biomechanical measures of neuromuscular control and valgus loading of the knee predict anterior cruciate ligament injury risk in female athletes. Am J Sports Med. 2005;33(4):492-501.
evidence.claim: Neuromuscular training incorporating balance and proprioceptive exercises reduced ACL injury risk by approximately 52%.

evidence.citation: Hébert-Losier K, Wessman C, Alricsson M, Svantesson U. Updated reliability and normative values for the standing heel-rise test in healthy adults. Physiotherapy. 2017;103(4):446-452.
evidence.claim: Single-leg calf raise endurance correlates with lower limb injury risk in runners; normative value for healthy adults is approximately 25 repetitions.

evidence.citation: Loudon JK, Wiesner D, Goist-Foley HL, Asjes C, Loudon KL. Intrarater reliability of functional performance tests for subjects with patellofemoral pain syndrome. J Athl Train. 2002;37(3):256-261.
evidence.claim: Single-leg wall sit and step-down tests demonstrate good intrarater reliability for assessing functional performance in patellofemoral pain populations.

evidence.citation: Piva SR, Fitzgerald K, Irrgang JJ, et al. Reliability of measures of impairments associated with patellofemoral pain syndrome. BMC Musculoskelet Disord. 2006;7:33.
evidence.claim: Step-down test from a 20cm step is a reliable measure of eccentric quadriceps control and correlates with patellofemoral pain severity.
